source_metadata.title_raw: Andrew Morris - A-19-059 - April 18, 2019 - Mammoth Lakes

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Andrew Morris 
Town Attorney 
Town of Mammoth Lakes 
P.O. Box 1609 
Mammoth Lakes, CA 93546 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-059 
Dear Mr. Morris: 
April 18, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Town of Mammoth Lakes 
Planning and Economic Development Commissioner Paul Chang regarding the conflict of interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice 
under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other any other conflict of interest 
laws. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in governmental decisions relating 
to the "Yotelpad Project," a project to construct 177 residential units, given that the Commissioner 
has an ownership interest in Shelter Distilling, a restaurant, bar, and distillery located within 
approximately 900 feet of the Yotelpad Project? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The Act prohibits the Commissioner from taking part in decisions relating to the 
Y otelpad Project because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a material 
financial effect on the Commissioner's interests in Shelter Distilling based on the facts presented. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-19-059 
Page No. 2 
Mammoth Lakes is a four-season resort community located in the eastern Sierra Nevada. 
The largest attraction in the Town of Mammoth Lakes is Mammoth Mountain, a ski and snowboard 
resort that also draws mountain bikers and other visitors in the summer. The Village Gondola 
provides access to one of Mammoth Mountain's three base lodges. The Village at Mammoth (the 
"Village") is a collection of restaurants and shops located at the base of the Village Gondola. 
You are the Mammoth Lakes Town Attorney and the authorized representative of Mammoth 
Lakes Planning and Economic Development Commissioner Paul Chang. The Town will soon 
consider the "Yotelpad Project," a project to construct 177 residential units (comprised of 156 
studios and 21 townhomes) on a project site located southeast of the Village on Minaret Road. 
Although the developer of the Y otelpad Project intends to sell all 177 units, it is anticipated that 
many of the units will be rented on a short-term basis to visitors to the Town, and that the Yotelpad 
Project may end up functioning much like a hotel. 
From the Yotelpad Project site, the closest and fastest route to access Mammoth Mountain is 
to head north on Minaret Road, then west through the Village, and then ust: tht: Villagt: Gumlula Lu 
ascend the Mountain. It is anticipated that most Y otelpad occupants will take this route to access, 
and will take the reverse route to return from, the Mountain, thereby increasing the total foot traffic 
through the Village. Although there are other bars and restaurants located nearby the Y otelpad 
Project site, the Village is the closest collection of restaurants and shops to it. 
The Commissioner has an ownership interest of less than 10 percent in Shelter Distilling, a 
restaurant, bar, and distillery that operates out of leased space in the Village. Shelter Distilling is 
located approximately 900 feet northwest of the Y otelpad Project site. 
ANALYSI~ 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a material 
financial effect distinguishable from the decision's effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 
and 87103.) An official's interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the 
Act are identified in Section 87103. With respect to decisions relating to the Yotelpad Project, the 
Commissioner has: a business interest in Shelter Distilling assuming his investment in that business 
entity is.worth $2,000 or more (see Section 87103(a)); a source of income interest in Shelter 
Distilling assuming he receives $500 or more in income from Shelter Distilling in the 12 months 
prior to the decision (see Section 87103(c); and an interest in his personal finances and those of 
immediate family members (see Section 87103).2 
2 Section 87103 (b) provides that an official has a real property interest in any real property in which he or she 
has an interest of $2,000 or more. Section 82033 sets forth the Act's definition of an "interest in real property," and 
provides that an individual's interests in real property include "a pro rata share of interests in real property of any 
business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a LO-
percent interest or greater." Because the Commissioner does not have a IO-percent interest or greater in Shelter 
Distilling, he does not have a real property interest in his pro rata share of Shelter Distilling's real property interest in its 
leased space in the Village. 


ForeseeabiJity and MateriaLity 
File No. A-19-059 
Page No. 3 
Regulation 1870l(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial 
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in 
the decision; an official's interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or the 
subject of, the decision; and an interest is the "subject of a proceeding" if the decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or 
contract with, the interest. Regulation 18701 (b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to a 
decision's effect on an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides 
that the decision's effect on such an interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." 
Because neither the Commissioner's interests in Shelter Distilling nor his interest in his 
personal finances and those of immediate family members is explicitly involved in decisions 
relating to the Yotelpad Project, those decisions' effect on each of those interests is 
reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. 
Regulation 18702. l(b) provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest in a 
privately-held business entity is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find 
it reasonably foreseeable that the decision's effect would contribute to a change in the value of that 
business entity.3 
The decisions relating to the Y otelpad Project may result in the construction of 177 
additional residential units nearby the Village, within approximately 900 feet of Shelter Distilling. 
Further, it is anticipated that the occupants of the Yotelpad Project will use the Village Gondola to 
access, and return from, Mammoth Mountain, thereby increasing total foot traffic through the 
Village. Therefore, the potential influx of Y otelpad Project occupants is likely to result in an 
increase in customers for Shelter Distilling. Thus, it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions 
relating to the Yotelpad Project would contribute to a change in the value of the Shelter Distilling 
based on the facts presented. 
Public Generally Exception 
Regulation 18703(a) sets forth the "Public Generally Exception" to the Act's conflict of 
interest provisions, and provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest is 
"indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official establishes that a significant 
segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her interest is not unique compared to the 
3 Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on an official's source 
of income interest, and subdivision (a)( 4) of that regulation provides that if the income is from the sale of goods or 
services and if the source of income is a business entity, materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1. 
Regulation 18702.5 sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision's effect on an official's interest in his or 
her personal finances and those of immediate family members, and subdivision (c) of that regulation provides that if the 
decision also affects the official's business interest, materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1. 
Therefore, we apply Regulation 18702.1 to determine if the decisions relating to the Yotelpad Project would have a 
disqualifying effect on the Commissioner's interests at issue. 


File No. A-19-059 
Page No. 4 
effect on the significant segment." The facts presented do not establish that the Public Generally 
Exception applies. 4 
Accordingly, the Act prohibits the Commissioner from taking part in decisions relating to 
the Yotelpad Project. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division 
4 You should seek additional advice if there are facts indicating that the Public Generally Exception may apply 
to the effect of decisions relating to the Yotelpad Project on the Commissioner's interests at issue.

financial effect on the Commissioner's interests in Shelter Distilling based on the facts presented.

QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in governmental decisions relating 
to the "Yotelpad Project," a project to construct 177 residential units, given that the Commissioner 
has an ownership interest in Shelter Distilling, a restaurant, bar, and distillery located within 
approximately 900 feet of the Yotelpad Project?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The Act prohibits the Commissioner from taking part in decisions relating to the 
Y otelpad Project because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a material 
financial effect on the Commissioner's interests in Shelter Distilling based on the facts presented.
